gold_note:
CHIEF COMPLAINT

Neck pain.

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Brandon Green is a pleasant 46-year-old male who presents to the clinic today for the evaluation of neck pain. His pain began when he was involved in a motor vehicle accident in 02/2022 when he was on his way home from a pain clinic. The patient notes that he has been in 4 motor vehicle accidents; however, he notes that he was fine after the first two accidents, but the third motor vehicle accident is when his neck and back pain began. He states that he was in therapy following the second accident and had surgery after his third accident. The patient was seen at a pain clinic secondary to neck and back pain. He was prescribed fentanyl; however, he has not received a prescription for several weeks. Today, he reports that his pain is a 12 out of 10. He describes his pain as sharp and incapacitating with stiffness and pain. The patient also reports headaches, occasional dizziness. He denies any recent visual disturbances. He also reports numbness in his left arm and right leg. The patient also reports spasms throughout his body. He states that he has been experiencing fatigue since the accident. He notes that he is unable to work with this much pain.

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Reports fatigue.
Eyes: Denies any recent visual disturbances.
Musculoskeletal: Reports neck and back pain, and occasional swelling and bruising of the neck.
Neurological: Reports headaches, dizziness, spasms, and numbness.

PHYSICAL EXAM

SKIN: No lacerations.
MSK: Examination of the cervical spine: Pain on palpation on the bony process and muscle. Moderate ROM. No bruising or edema noted.

RESULTS

X-rays of the neck reveal no fractures.

ASSESSMENT

Neck sprain.

PLAN

After reviewing the patient's examination and radiographic findings today, I have had a lengthy discussion with the patient in regards to his current symptoms. I have explained to him that his x-rays did not reveal any signs of a fracture. I recommended an MRI for further evaluation. I have also prescribed the patient Robaxin 1500 mg every 6 to 8 hours to treat his pain. I have also advised him to utilize ice, a heating pad, IcyHot, or Biofreeze on his neck as needed. I have also provided him with a home exercise program to work on his range of motion. I advised the patient that he will not be able to work until we have the MRI results.

INSTRUCTIONS

The patient will follow up with me after his MRI for results.

Subjective:

He reports experiencing sharp, incapacitating pain that makes it difficult for him to move his neck and perform daily activities. He has a history of multiple car crashes, including one that required surgery, and has been to therapy. He has also been prescribed fentanyl for pain management, but has not received a prescription for several weeks. He denies any fever, headache, dizziness, visual disturbances, or numbness, but reports experiencing fatigue since the accident. He has been experiencing bruising and swelling in the area, but no laceration is present. He is currently taking no medications other than possibly fentanyl, which he has not received a prescription for in several weeks.

Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]

Physical exam: The patient has pain on palpation of the neck, both on the bony process and the muscle. He has a moderate range of movement in his neck, but reports pain when bending forward and backwards. He denies any bruising or swelling on examination, but reports experiencing bruising and swelling in the area.

Test Results: The patient had an x-ray before coming in, which showed no fracture.

Assessment / Problem List:

Problem list: Neck sprain; suspected fracture (status: suspected); past medical history of multiple car crashes; history of fentanyl use (status: past).

Plan:
I prescribed robaxin 1500mg orally every 6-8 hours as needed for muscle relaxation and pain management. I referred the patient to physical therapy to evaluate additional steps for pain management. I also ordered an MRI to further evaluate the extent of the injury. I will refer the patient to pain medication if the robaxin is not effective in managing his pain. I will give the patient a note to take off work due to his condition.

Follow-up: The patient will return for follow-up after the MRI results are available, which will be in 1-2 weeks.

Subjective:
The patient, a male, presents with a complaint of neck pain. He reports experiencing pain since a car crash in February of this year, which he estimates to be the third car crash he has been in recently. He describes the pain as sharp and incapacitating, rating it a 12 out of 10. He reports difficulty moving his neck, stiffness, and pain on palpation on both the bony process and muscle. He also reports experiencing headaches, dizziness, numbness in his left arm and right leg, and muscle spasms. He denies visual disturbances and fatigue, but admits to feeling tired since the accident. He mentions that his lawyer told him to come to this office and that he has been to a pain clinic and a therapist. He is currently taking fentanyl, but has not received a prescription for several weeks.

Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure, pulse rate, oxygen saturation, and temperature were not mentioned.
Physical exam: The patient's neck pain is exacerbated by palpation on both the bony process and muscle. He has a moderate range of motion in his neck, but pain on bending forward and backwards. No bruising, swelling, or laceration were observed.

Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient has a neck sprain.
Problem list: Neck sprain; active.

Plan:
I prescribed Robaxin 1500mg orally every 6-8 hours for pain management. I will refer the patient to physical therapy and we will obtain an MRI to further evaluate the extent of the injury. I will also refer the patient to pain medication if the Robaxin does not provide adequate relief. If the MRI results indicate that local injections are an option, we will explore this further.

Follow-up:
The patient will follow up with me after the MRI results are available. If the patient's symptoms persist, we will re-evaluate the treatment plan. The patient will be given a note excusing them from work until the MRI results are available.
